fields.description:
Activity-based liveness for LLM gate ops using mechanisms compatible with the runner's synchronous `agent.run_sync()` architecture: a generous per-request read timeout + a per-tool timeout + step caps — and NO total-runtime timeout. The stream-event idle-watchdog is explicitly DEFERRED (it needs an async runner).

## Why
A single hard total-runtime timeout truncates healthy multi-minute / large-context (1M-token) runs; liveness must be gauged by activity, not total elapsed time. BUT the runner uses `agent.run_sync()` exclusively (`src/rebar/llm/runner.py:323,461,474`) — a blocking synchronous call that exposes no concurrent stream-event hooks. The LangGraph `idle_timeout` / Claude Agent SDK stall-watchdog pattern requires async `agent.run()` / `run_stream_events` and an event loop, which this synchronous runner does not have. So the correct, in-architecture design achieves activity-based liveness at request+tool granularity WITHOUT asyncio.

## What
- **Single-turn / structured calls:** a generous but finite per-request read timeout via `httpx.Timeout(read=...)` on the model's http client (sized above worst-case large-context prefill) + `request_limit=2`.
- **Agentic calls (run_sync-compatible liveness):** the per-request read timeout (bounds a hung model call — the real failure) + a **per-tool execution timeout** (`Agent(tool_timeout=...)`, bounds a hung tool) + **step caps** (`request_limit`, `tool_calls_limit`, optional `total_tokens_limit`, bounding infinite/oscillating loops). Between "hung request", "hung tool", and "infinite loop", a healthy long run is never truncated and a genuinely stalled/zombie run is always caught — with no total-runtime timeout and no event loop.
- **Deferred (ADR + follow-on ticket, NOT built here):** the sub-event stream-idle-watchdog (reset-on-stream-event) — it requires migrating the agentic runner to async `agent.run()` / `run_stream_events`, which conflicts with the exclusive `run_sync` design. Capture the decision + the async-migration trigger in an ADR; file a discovered_from follow-on. If ever built, wrap the event iterator to avoid pydantic-ai #4796 (failing-tool + handler `AssertionError` masks the real error).
- Tuning invariant: the `read` timeout must be >= worst-case single-request time-to-first-token (a 1M-token prefill can be minutes of silence) — a too-tight read timeout is the truncation bug in miniature.

## Scope
- Per-execution_mode timeout wiring (per-request read + per-tool timeout + step caps); config keys `verify.llm_request_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_tool_timeout_s`, `verify.llm_agentic_step_limit`.
- NOT in scope: any total-runtime cap (rejected — truncates healthy runs); the async stream-event watchdog (deferred to ADR/follow-on).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A generous per-request read timeout is set for both modes (as `httpx.Timeout`), sized for large-context prefill, and configurable; verified by an injected slow-but-live model that returns just under the read timeout and is NOT aborted.
- [ ] Agentic runs enforce a per-tool timeout + step caps (`request_limit`/`tool_calls_limit`); an injected hung tool trips the tool timeout, and a looping model trips the step cap — each surfaces a classified failure.
- [ ] A hung model request (no bytes past the read timeout) is aborted and classified; no total-runtime timeout exists anywhere in the gate path (asserted by test).
- [ ] The whole timeout mechanism runs under `agent.run_sync()` with no asyncio introduced (verified — no new event loop).
- [ ] An ADR records the deferred async stream-event watchdog + its migration trigger; a discovered_from follow-on ticket exists.
- [ ] `make test`/`make lint`/`make typecheck` green.

Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`, `src/rebar/config.py`, `src/rebar/_config_schema.py`, `docs/adr/`.
